text: Hi, I'm Abu Bakr Ali. I'm showing a case here of sigmoid colectomy for cancer in a morbidly obese patient. BMI was a fifty-five.

The surgeon attempts circumferential dissection around the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), aiming to harvest lymph nodes from the surrounding fatty tissue. A lymph node bleed is visible during the skeletonization of the IMA.

I like using the Maryland for dissection. I always like, I always use lighted catheters. A lot of people like using ICG. I like using lighted catheters personally. They're more expensive, but I like them.

The surgeon attempts to elevate lymph nodes with the specimen, maneuvering around the artery. Using electrocautery, the surgeon alternates between above and below the artery before transecting, resulting in visible bleeding.
The surgeon attempts to control bleeding using a vessel sealer, while the camera view becomes smudged. Pressure is applied to the bleeding site, and a clip-a-plier is requested for further control.
The surgeon performs a figure-eight suture using a 3-0 Prolene suture, ensuring the short tail is locked to prevent it from being pulled out, effectively controlling the situation.
The surgeon staples the tissue, navigating through a significant amount of adipose tissue. The stapling process proceeds smoothly despite the challenging conditions.

The surgeon performs a leak test by observing for bubbling near the staple line. Initially, no bubbling is seen, but upon closer inspection next to the silk suture, bubbling is observed, indicating a potential leak.
